A painted milepost stands beside a wagon rut, and the ditch beyond it is too quiet. Bandits rise from the grass when the party slows to read the sign.
Armed toughs have dragged a log across the road and call it a lawful toll. Their smiles fade when challenged.
A stone culvert under the road hums with thin wings. Stirges pour out when hooves or boots drum across the hollow span.
Rain has smeared the painted arrows on a fork marker. Comparing wheel ruts and old camp ash reveals which branch sees real traffic.
A small purse lies pressed into a muddy wagon rut, almost hidden under straw. The contents are damp but spendable.
A bridge board lifts underfoot with a rotten crack. A quick shift of weight keeps the crossing from becoming a plunge into the ditch below.
An overturned cart blocks one wheel track, its driver gone and its cargo scattered. Fresh footprints lead away from the road at a run.
A new detour sign points toward a narrow lane that does not appear on any old route list. The paint is fresh, and so are the bootprints around it.
A messenger waves from beside a lame horse, clutching a sealed packet. Delivering it onward could matter more than reaching camp on time.
Berries ripen in the hedgerow beside the road, dusty but sweet. The best clusters hang just beyond thorny branches.
Travelers have trampled most of the verge, but hardy herbs still grow around a milestone. Cutting them cleanly avoids grit and road filth.
A torn sack has spilled grain under the wagon tracks. Quick sorting saves enough clean kernels before birds and mud take the rest.
A rain-filled ditch beside the road wriggles with trapped minnows. They are easy to net before the puddle drains into the culvert.
A deep pool below a bridge holds trout in the shadow of the span. Dropping a line from above spooks them unless handled gently.
Crayfish hide among stones at a shallow ford where wagons slow. The catch is good if the water is not churned first.
Loose chips of pale stone lie where an old milestone was carved nearby. A little searching finds larger usable pieces in the roadside bank.
A roadcut exposes red clay beneath roots and gravel. It can be gathered quickly before traffic shakes more dirt into it.
Flint nodules turn up where wagon wheels bite through thin soil. Sorting sharp useful pieces from broken gravel takes care.
A roadside tree has fallen across the verge after a storm. Its trunk is clear enough to cut before traffic needs the space.
A wrecked wagon has been pushed into the ditch, its wheels gone but its boards still useful. Pulling nails takes longer than cutting wood.
A cracked signpost leans where a newer marker has replaced it. The dry post can be salvaged without confusing travelers.
